section.title: Fools, Sluggards, and Gossips
section.sectionRationale: Part of the Hezekiah collection, 25:1 to 29:27, this chapter groups its similes with unusual thematic tightness around three character types: the fool, whom honor does not fit, the sluggard, paralyzed by his own excuses, and the gossip, whose words are fuel for strife, closing with the hidden malice of a deceitful heart.
section.thesis: Honor is as unfitting for a fool as snow in summer, the sluggard's excuses reveal a self-deceived paralysis, and without gossip a quarrel dies out, since hatred that hides behind smooth speech will finally be exposed before everyone.
section.frameNote: Four clusters: honor unfitting a fool, and the paradox of answering folly; the fool's mouth, the fool's honor, and the danger of hiring a fool; the sluggard's excuses and endless turning; the gossip who kindles strife, and the deceit that hides hatred.

verses.text: Like snow in summer and rain at harvest, honor does not befit a {{kesil|fool}}.
Like a fluttering sparrow or darting swallow, an undeserved curse does not come to rest.
A whip for the horse, a bridle for the donkey, and a rod for the backs of fools!
Do not answer a fool according to his folly, or you yourself will be like him.
Answer a fool according to his folly, lest he become wise in his own eyes.

units.label: The fool's mouth and honor
units.note: hiring a fool, honoring a fool

verses.text: Like cutting off one's own feet or drinking violence is the sending of a message by the hand of a fool.
Like lame legs hanging limp is a proverb in the mouth of a fool.
Like binding a stone into a sling is the giving of honor to a fool.
Like a thorn that falls into the hand of a drunkard is a proverb in the mouth of a fool.
Like an archer who wounds at random is he who hires a fool or passerby.
As a dog returns to its vomit, so a fool repeats his folly.
Do you see a man who is wise in his own eyes? There is more hope for a fool than for him.

units.label: The sluggard's excuses
units.note: the door turns on its hinges

verses.text: The slacker says, "A lion is in the road! A fierce lion roams the public square!"
As a door turns on its hinges, so the slacker turns on his bed.
The slacker buries his hand in the dish; it wearies him to bring it back to his mouth.
The slacker is wiser in his own eyes than seven men who answer discreetly.
Like one who grabs a dog by the ears is a passerby who meddles in a quarrel not his own.
Like a madman shooting firebrands and deadly arrows,
so is the man who deceives his neighbor and says, "I was only joking!"
Without wood, a fire goes out; without {{ragan|gossip}}, a conflict ceases.
Like charcoal for embers and wood for fire, so is a quarrelsome man for kindling strife.
The words of a gossip are like choice morsels that go down into the inmost being.
Like glaze covering an earthen vessel are burning lips and a wicked heart.
A hateful man disguises himself with his speech, but he lays up deceit in his heart.
When he speaks graciously, do not believe him, for seven abominations fill his heart.
Though his hatred is concealed by deception, his wickedness will be exposed in the assembly.
He who digs a pit will fall into it, and he who rolls a stone will have it roll back on him.
A lying tongue hates those it crushes, and a flattering mouth causes ruin.

kesil.gloss: Fool, the stubborn, self-satisfied person who resists correction. Verse 1 says honor fits him as poorly as snow fits summer, an image of things fundamentally out of season and out of place, capturing how deeply mismatched status and folly are.

ragan.gloss: Gossip, whisperer, one who stirs conflict through repeated talk. Verse 20 says a conflict ceases without this kind of talebearing, just as a fire dies without wood, showing gossip is not a passive habit but active fuel that keeps quarrels burning.

basic.context: This chapter is unusual for how tightly its similes cluster around three character types in turn: the fool, whom honor never fits and whose folly is as compulsive as a dog returning to its vomit, the sluggard, whose lion-in-the-road excuses hide sheer paralysis, and the gossip, whose fuel keeps every quarrel burning, and whose hidden hatred will one day be exposed before everyone.
basic.hermeneutics: The chapter teaches almost entirely through vivid simile, and famously offers what looks like a direct contradiction, verses 4 and 5, do not answer a fool according to his folly, then answer a fool according to his folly. Read together rather than separately, they teach discernment: sometimes silence protects you from becoming like the fool, sometimes a pointed answer prevents the fool from imagining he has won, and wisdom knows which moment calls for which response.
basic.originalLanguages: Kesil, fool, verse 1, is pictured as someone honor fits as poorly as snow fits summer, capturing not stupidity so much as a fundamental mismatch of character and status. Nirgan, gossip or whisperer, verse 20, names one whose repeated talk is the literal fuel of conflict, so that removing this fuel, not merely staying silent about it, is what allows a quarrel to finally die.
basic.typology: The exposure of hidden hatred in the assembly, verse 26, anticipates the final judgment where all hidden things are brought to light, 1 Corinthians 4:5. The pit dug for another that swallows its own digger, verse 27, echoes the pattern fulfilled at the cross, where the schemes of Jesus' enemies became the very means of His victory over them.
basic.christ: Verse 26's promise that concealed hatred will finally be exposed in the assembly anticipates the New Testament's teaching that God will bring every hidden thing to light and disclose the purposes of the heart, 1 Corinthians 4:5, a judgment only Christ can execute justly because He alone sees hearts perfectly. And verse 27's image of a man falling into the pit he dug for another echoes, in miniature, the great reversal of the cross, where the very plot to destroy Jesus became, in God's hands, the means of defeating sin and death themselves.
basic.god: God's justice is patient but certain, since hidden hatred behind smooth speech, verse 26, will finally be exposed, and self-destructive schemes, digging pits and rolling stones meant for others, verse 27, tend to recoil on their authors even within this life. He gives wisdom the discernment to know when silence and when a pointed answer best serves truth, verses 4 to 5, rather than a single rule for every encounter with folly.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Corinthians 4:5 echoes the exposure of hidden things promised in verse 26. Galatians 6:7 echoes the certainty of consequence in digging a pit for another, verse 27. Psalm 7:15-16 pictures the wicked falling into the very pit they dug, paralleling verse 27. James 3:5-6 develops the destructive fire imagery of gossip and the tongue in verses 20-21.
basic.oneStory: Proverbs 26 exposes the fool who cannot be honored, the sluggard paralyzed by excuse, and the gossip whose fuel keeps quarrels alive and whose hidden hatred will one day be exposed. The whole story of Scripture answers with the promise that God will finally bring every hidden thing to light, and with Christ, whose enemies' own schemes became the means of their defeat and His victory.

context.literary: Chapter 26 belongs to the Hezekiah collection, 25:1 to 29:27, and is unusually tightly organized by subject, with verses 1-12 devoted to the fool, 13-16 to the sluggard, and 17-28 to the gossip and the deceitful, contentious heart, a structure more deliberate than most Proverbs chapters.
context.historical: In a small, close-knit community where reputation and word of mouth carried enormous weight, gossip and false 'I was only joking' deceit, verse 19, posed genuine social danger, capable of destroying relationships and community trust as surely as a madman's firebrands.
context.canonical: The famous seeming contradiction of verses 4 and 5 has long been noted as a deliberate teaching device, showing that biblical wisdom sometimes holds two true statements in tension rather than reducing complex situations to a single formula, a pattern also visible in the book of Ecclesiastes.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To expose, through vivid and sometimes deliberately paired similes, the folly of the fool, the paralysis of the sluggard, and the destructive power of gossip and concealed hatred, training the reader to discern wisely rather than apply mechanical rules.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: Most of the chapter describes character types through simile rather than issuing direct commands, though verses like 20, without gossip a conflict ceases, imply the reader should refuse to fuel strife with talebearing, a real, if indirectly stated, prescription.

debates.question: How can verses 4 and 5 both be true, 'do not answer a fool according to his folly' and 'answer a fool according to his folly'?

views.label: The verses contradict each other and simply preserve two competing traditional sayings
views.case: Some read the juxtaposition as evidence of an editor collecting variant proverbs without resolving their tension, since Hebrew wisdom literature often preserves diverse voices side by side.

views.label: The verses teach discernment about differing situations, not a contradiction
views.case: Each verse names a different danger, becoming like the fool through pointless argument, verse 4, versus letting the fool imagine he has won through silence, verse 5; read together they train wisdom to discern which danger is greater in a given moment rather than applying one rule mechanically.

debates.bearing: Reading the pair as teaching discernment rather than contradiction fits the genre of Proverbs as a whole, which trains wise judgment for varied situations rather than delivering one-size-fits-all formulas, and it models exactly the kind of contextual wisdom the book calls its readers to develop.

study.originalLanguages: Kesil, fool, verse 1, is not merely unintelligent but fundamentally out of step, so that honor fits him as poorly as snow fits summer. Nirgan, gossip, verse 20, describes an active fuel for conflict, someone whose repeated talk keeps quarrels burning the way wood keeps a fire alive, so that removing the talk, not merely ignoring it, is what finally lets strife die.

typology.type: Hidden hatred finally exposed in the assembly, 26:26
typology.fulfillment: The final judgment where God brings hidden things to light and discloses the purposes of hearts, 1 Corinthians 4:5.

typology.type: Falling into the pit dug for another, 26:27
typology.fulfillment: The cross, where the schemes of Christ's enemies became the very means of His victory over sin and death.

study.christ: Verse 26's confidence that concealed hatred will eventually be exposed before the assembly anticipates the New Testament's teaching that the Lord will bring to light what is hidden and disclose the purposes of the heart, 1 Corinthians 4:5, a judgment that belongs finally to Christ, who alone sees every heart perfectly. And verse 27's picture of a man falling into his own dug pit finds its grandest, most redemptive echo at the cross, where the plots of Jesus' enemies to destroy Him became, astonishingly, the very means by which God accomplished salvation and defeated the powers that thought they had won.
study.god: God's justice moves patiently but surely; hidden hatred behind smooth speech will finally come to light, verse 26, and schemes meant to harm others tend to recoil upon their authors even in the ordinary course of life, verse 27. God also grants wisdom the discernment to recognize when silence and when a direct answer best serves truth in the face of folly, verses 4-5, refusing to reduce wise living to a single mechanical rule.

crossReferences.note: God will disclose the purposes of hearts, fulfilling the exposure of hidden hatred in 26:26.
A man reaps what he sows, echoing the pit that swallows its own digger in 26:27.
The wicked man falls into the pit he made, paralleling 26:27.
The tongue is a fire, developing the destructive gossip imagery of 26:20-21.

study.oneStory: Proverbs 26 exposes folly that resists correction, sloth that hides behind excuse, and gossip that fuels quarrels while concealing hatred behind smooth speech. The Bible's larger story answers with the promise that God will finally expose every hidden thing, and with Christ, whose enemies' schemes against Him became, in God's hands, the very means of victory over sin and death.

quiz.q: What does verse 1 compare honor given to a fool to?

options.text: Snow in summer and rain at harvest, things fundamentally out of place.
options.note: Honor is pictured as deeply mismatched with a fool's character.

options.text: Apples of gold in silver settings.
options.note: That image, from 25:11, describes fitly spoken words, not honor given to a fool.

options.text: A well-timed and welcome gift.
options.note: The verse's point is mismatch and unfittingness, not welcome timing.

options.text: Rain during the normal rainy season.
options.note: The verse specifies rain at harvest time, when it is unwelcome and out of season, not during the normal rainy season.

quiz.q: How should the seeming tension between verses 4 and 5 be best understood?

options.text: As teaching discernment: different dangers call for either silence or a pointed answer.
options.note: Wisdom knows which response fits which situation rather than applying one rule always.

options.text: As a scribal error that should be corrected.
options.note: The text is intentional, not an error to be fixed.

options.text: As proof that Proverbs contains unreliable, contradictory teaching.
options.note: This misunderstands the genre and purpose of the juxtaposition.

options.text: As meaning you should always answer a fool according to his folly.
options.note: This ignores verse 4's equally valid warning against becoming like the fool through pointless argument.

quiz.q: According to verse 20, what happens to conflict without gossip?

options.text: It ceases, just as a fire goes out without wood.
options.note: Gossip is pictured as the active fuel that keeps quarrels burning.

options.text: It intensifies without gossip to distract from it.
options.note: This is the opposite of the verse's point.

options.text: It remains exactly the same regardless of gossip.
options.note: The verse specifically ties conflict's continuation to gossip's presence.

options.text: It slowly fades over a period of years.
options.note: The verse's image, a fire going out without wood, suggests a more immediate cessation, not a slow multi-year fade.

quiz.q: What does verse 24-25 warn about a hateful man's gracious speech?

options.text: Do not believe him, for he disguises his speech while laying up deceit in his heart.
options.note: Smooth, gracious words can mask genuine hatred and deception.

options.text: Gracious speech always reflects a truly changed heart.
options.note: This is exactly what the passage warns against assuming.

options.text: Gracious speech is a minor concern compared to actions.
options.note: The passage treats deceptive gracious speech as a serious matter in itself, not a minor one.

options.text: Gracious speech should be believed unless proven otherwise later.
options.note: The passage's counsel is immediate caution, 'do not believe him,' not a wait-and-see approach.

quiz.q: What does verse 27 say happens to one who digs a pit for another?

options.text: He will fall into it himself.
options.note: Schemes meant to harm others tend to recoil on their authors.

options.text: He successfully traps his intended victim every time.
options.note: This is the opposite of what the verse promises.

options.text: Nothing happens to him at all.
options.note: The verse specifically describes a consequence for the schemer.

options.text: He is only punished if caught by others.
options.note: The verse describes an inherent, near-automatic consequence, not one contingent on being caught.

questions: Verses 4 and 5 teach that wisdom sometimes calls for silence and sometimes for a direct answer. Think of a recent conflict with a foolish or stubborn person. Which response did you actually need, and which did you give?
The sluggard invents a lion in the road to avoid work, verse 13. What excuse are you currently using to avoid a responsibility you know you should face?
Verse 20 says a conflict dies without gossip to fuel it. Is there a quarrel or tension you have been unintentionally keeping alive through repeating what you have heard?
Verses 24-25 warn against trusting gracious words that mask a hateful heart. Have you ever been fooled by smooth speech, or have you used smooth speech to mask your own resentment?
Verse 27 promises that pits dug for others tend to swallow their diggers. Is there a scheme, grudge, or manipulation you are engaged in that this proverb should make you reconsider?

translationNotes.note: The juxtaposition of seemingly opposite commands is widely regarded as intentional, not contradictory, teaching that wise responses to folly depend on discerning the specific situation.
'Like binding a stone into a sling' pictures rendering a weapon useless or even dangerous to the user, since honoring a fool similarly misfires and can backfire on the one who grants it.

'Glaze covering an earthen vessel' pictures a cheap clay pot given an attractive silvery coating, an image of surface beauty hiding a common, even worthless interior, matching smooth speech over a wicked heart.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the Makor scripture toolkit for Proverbs 26. No em or en dashes used anywhere in this file.
